summary: A misrep document describes an ISR aircraft mission over Iraq/Syria supporting operation Phantom Flex, collecting SIGINT and FMV surveillance from an OKAS-based sortie between 0542Z and 0036Z, with times and personnel/asset identifiers redacted; the record does not describe any anomalous aerial object.
The document shows only a section heading '1.4(a)' with no accompanying narrative or descriptive text visible, providing no reportable details about an observed object.
An imaging frame with crosshair overlay shows a small dark rounded object near the center, with no accompanying descriptive text visible in the field of view.
The document displays only a section heading reading '1.4(a)' with no accompanying descriptive text or content visible.

This is a 1996 RTI final report for the Air Force 30th and 45th Space Wing safety offices modeling unlikely space-booster failures (Mode-5 responses) in risk calculations, including a history of Atlas, Delta, and Titan launch anomalies through August 1996. It is a technical/administrative report and does not describe an anomalous aerial object.

This is an administrative front matter and distribution list for a 30th Space Wing history office document summarizing rocket launch operations from Vandenberg Air Force Base, California, from 1958 to 2000.

An FBI Knoxville field office file references reported 'flying saucers' observed over the Oak Ridge, Tennessee area in July 1947, with enclosures including two photographs and a newspaper clipping from the Knoxville News-Sentinel.
The document consists solely of a section header labeled '1.4(a)' with no accompanying descriptive text or details about any observed object provided in the image.

An email exchange between an intelligence data analysis technician and an information disclosure analyst confirms that two tearlines dated 10APR25 and 11APR25 are approved at the UNCLASSIFIED level. Both tearlines report a US aircraft observing one possible UAP for approximately 12 and 23 seconds respectively, at unknown altitude and speed, with no interference noted.

A 1955 US Air Force Air Intelligence report describes an account from three US travelers (including a US Senator, an Army officer, and a committee consultant) who reported observing two round unconventional aircraft ascend near-vertically from the ground in the Trans-Caucasus region of the USSR, hovering with rotating outer surfaces before accelerating northward at increased speed and altitude near a rail line.

A 50 ATKS mission report (MISREP) describes an ISR aircraft sortie over the eastern Mediterranean and Syria, during which the aircraft was reacted to by 1-3 aircraft including a possible RJAF Su-30, observed a probable Su-27/35 landing at Al Assad airfield, and reported one possible small unidentified aerial phenomenon at 0117Z.

Apollo mission transcript (Tape 5) records crew members reporting bright, jagged fragments or particles drifting past their windows during and after a spacecraft maneuver, discussing possible causes such as ice chunks, paint flaking, or debris from the S-IVB stage.
A sensor or scope display with crosshair reticle and timestamp shows a small dark dot near the center, with surrounding text areas redacted; no further descriptive detail is legible.

During the Gemini 7 mission, astronaut Borman reported a 'bogey' at ten o'clock high and numerous small particles moving past the spacecraft at an estimated three to four miles distance, in addition to the sighting of the spent booster tumbling nearby. NASA public affairs later characterized the third unidentified object referenced in the exchange as the reported bogey.
An infrared or thermal targeting image with crosshair overlay shows a small dark object on terrain, with no accompanying descriptive text visible in the frame.

A checklist report describes an incident near Norcatur, Kansas on 18 February 1948, based on a newspaper account from an editor, describing a bluish-white smoke smudge with a mushroom shape at an estimated altitude of 30-35 miles, accompanied by one large explosion followed by smaller ones, and disintegrating afterward.

This is a NASA Apollo 17 crew debriefing on science observations, including a scientist discussing UV background surveys, Coma cluster hydrogen searches, Lyman-alpha hydrogen radiation studies, and Earth spectrum observations; it does not describe any anomalous aerial object.

A July 1952 memorandum records that General Samford, Air Force A-2, told an inquirer that flying saucer phenomena remained a complete enigma but had been reported for over 100 years, with increased reporting attributed to improved observation methods and publicity. The memo notes that credible observers reported incredible things and that the Air Force treated the phenomena as a concern only because they were not understood, with little support given to theories of man-made or controlled origin.

An aircrew member reported a round object tracked via IR sensor over the Gulf of Aden on 04SEP2020, observed at 23,819 ft for about 8 minutes, described as moving at 168 degrees and 277 mph with several abrupt directional changes.

This State Department weekly political blotter from Mexico City covers domestic Mexican political events including a MORENA presidential candidate dispute, INE election commission appointments, and Mexico City security leadership changes; it does not describe any aerial phenomenon or object sighting.
A sensor image with a timestamp shows a small dark object near the center crosshair, with surrounding text fields redacted; no further descriptive detail is legible.
The image shows a targeting or tracking display with a crosshair reticle and a small spherical object below center, with no accompanying witness or contextual text visible.

An FBI FD-302 report describes a contractor who, while conducting LiDAR tests near a restricted airspace site in September 2023, saw a linear metallic/gray object with a bright white banded light on its east side. The object was reportedly larger than a drone but smaller than a 737, moved east to west at roughly 5000 feet, and disappeared after five to ten seconds.

An Apollo 17 crew debriefing describes Evans observing a bright fireball with a tunnel-like effect and a bright central spot visible through the rendezvous window, and Schmitt reporting recurring light flashes seen throughout the mission, including one thought to be on the lunar surface.

An FBI Los Angeles field office memo to the Director reports on a magazine, 'Flying Saucers International,' the journal of the Amalgamated Flying Saucer Clubs of America, forwarded by the Philadelphia office because a subscriber believed an article promoted Communist Party views. The article was allegedly written by a figure claiming to represent an extraterrestrial planet and received via short-wave radio by an individual named Bob Renaud; the Bureau's indices had no information on him and no investigation was pursued.

A USCENTCOM mission report (Misrep 9381202) describes an aircraft conducting ISR/FMV-SIGINT tasking over multiple locations, during which the crew observed an unidentified aerial phenomenon at coordinates 37D ST 69 07, referencing an associated UAP report for further detail.

A sensor still time-stamped 12/31/99 18:20:08 shows two small dark spots near the crosshair center of the frame, with measurement markers indicating a 3-unit scale on either side, but no additional descriptive text is visible due to redaction.
An analytic image shows a targeting reticle overlaid on aerial/satellite-style terrain with a small dark spot marked at the crosshair, with no accompanying descriptive text visible.

An FBI teletype from Savannah reports that AEC security at the Savannah River Plant relayed an account from two Du Pont Company employees who saw a blue light with an orange fringe, shaped like a saucer, flying over the plant's 400 area at high speed in a northeastern direction on the night of August 8, 1952.

A 482ATKS mission report (Misrep 4782130) describes an unmanned aircraft conducting SIGINT/ISR support over the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman, during which the crew reported observing a UAP at 1732Z, with details referred to a separate observation log line.

An FBI office memo from Detroit reports that a caller named David Weaver telephoned early on April 17, 1958, to describe seeing a circular object with a crystal-like dome that reflected lights, moving northward across the city near Six Mile and Lamphere Street. The report notes he tried unsuccessfully to reach Selfridge Air Force Base before contacting the FBI office.

A series of 1947 U.S. Air Materiel Command memos discusses continued reports of 'flying discs' from qualified observers and forwards these reports to Air Force headquarters. A follow-up directive establishes an official project (code named 'Sign') to collect, evaluate, and distribute information on aerial sighting reports of concern to national security.

A Misrep document describes a 20.3-hour Air Force ISR mission over the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman on behalf of NAVCENT, collecting SIGINT and IMINT via an airhandler platform, noting that observed noteworthy activity was recorded separately in an observation line not included in this excerpt.

FBI Detroit field office administrative papers referencing notes from a November 1957 interview with Wladyslaw Krasuski, with no descriptive content about any aerial phenomenon visible on these pages.

A calibration/reference display shows a crosshair grid with timestamp overlay; multiple black redaction bars obscure portions of the frame along both sides, with no discernible object described in the visible content.

A 389th EFS mission report describes a 2-ship F-15E flight conducting defensive counter air near Shaddadi, Syria, during which crews observed 3 possible unidentified aerial phenomena at FL240 and later a possible balloon at FL210; no threat reaction was fired and no further tracking resulted.

An enlarged photographic analysis presents five cropped detail areas showing bright light artifacts against dark backgrounds, overlaid on a lunar surface photograph with equipment tracks and scattered rocks. The document does not state an observer, location, or date beyond the lunar imagery context, and describes the artifacts only as small points or clusters of light.

An Air Force MISREP describes an aircraft flying an ISR mission near Baghdad, Iraq, collecting SIGINT and supporting operations, during which the crew reported observing a possible unidentified aerial phenomenon; details of the sighting itself are referenced in a separate UAP line not included in this excerpt.

This USCENTCOM mission report describes an aircraft sortie that collected SIGINT and supported NAVCENT operations near the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman. During the mission the crew received a guard call and observed one unknown formation, logged as a single observation entry, with no further description of its appearance or behavior.
A sensor display captures a small dark object near the crosshair reticle, with timestamp 12/31/99 18:19:06; contextual data around the frame has been redacted.

An Eleventh Air Force forwarding letter relays an FBI-sourced report of a round, phosphorescent object seen by New York Central railroad employees near Hobson, Ohio on the night of May 8, 1948, reportedly moving at great speed at an altitude of 6-8 miles and leaving a phosphorescent trail.

A 1985 U.S. Embassy Port Moresby cable reports that Papua New Guinea's National Intelligence Organization inquired about sightings of high-altitude, high-speed aircraft-like objects with lights and contrails observed on the night of January 24, including a pilot's radar contact and multiple ground witnesses; U.S. officials stated they had no record of B-52 or other U.S. aircraft in PNG airspace that day.

A heavily redacted Air Force misreport (Misrep 8799515) documents an incident, but nearly all narrative details including the aircraft, personnel, and object description have been withheld under exemption (b)(1)1.4a.

A multi-page declassified field report describes a joint helicopter search operation investigating earlier eyewitness reports of orbs, lights, and ground impact sounds near a mountain range. Personnel aboard the helicopter and at a listening post repeatedly observed orbs and swarms of lights that flared up and down in formations, hovered near the aircraft, moved at high speed, and appeared to interact with military aircraft in the area over several hours.

Skylab 1/2 crew debriefing transcript in which astronauts Kerwin, Conrad, and Weitz discuss recurring light flashes, spots, and streaks seen mostly with eyes closed or in peripheral vision during the mission, along with medical kit usage.

A sensor still with a timestamp shows two small dark blob-like objects near the frame center against a grey background, with a calibration crosshair overlay and multiple redacted text fields surrounding it. No shape detail or contextual description is legible to characterize the object further.

An imaging frame with crosshair overlay shows a small dark dot near center against a grainy grey background, with heavy black redaction blocks along the top and side margins obscuring surrounding context.

A USCENTCOM mission report (Misrep 10194673) describes a 124 ATKS aircraft mission over multiple days in July 2024, during which the crew reported observing one unidentified aerial phenomenon at approximately 140517Z JUL24 near a specified grid location, alongside routine ISR target development tasking.

A 1947 Washington Post clipping reports that a Catholic priest in Grafton, Wisconsin heard a swishing and whirring noise followed by a thud and mild explosion, then found a round metal disc about 18 inches in diameter and one-eighth inch thick in his yard, which he turned over to the FBI. The article notes the disc was warm, weighed four to five pounds, had a hole with wires and gadgets inside, and situates the discovery amid other nationwide reports of flying discs.

A sensor or scope display shows crosshair grid overlays with timestamp 12/31/99 18:11:19, but the main content areas including the object itself are redacted with black bars, leaving no visible object description.

This appears to be a sensor or targeting display grid with a central crosshair and reticle markings, redacted along the top and sides, with no discernible object or narrative content visible in the frame.

A 33 SOS mission report (Misrep 9329374) describes an ISR aircraft conducting FMV/SIGINT collection over a masked location; at 0035Z the crew observed one possible unidentified aerial phenomenon, referenced as UAP 1 in an attached but unincluded exhibit.

A 2023 USAF misrep describes a two-ship F-16CM flight out of Prince Sultan Air Base flying a defensive counter-air mission over Iraq and Syria; during the mission the crew reported observing multiple possible unidentified aerial phenomena before continuing the mission and returning to base without further incident.

This is Apollo 12 mission transcript covering communications between mission control and the crew, including a lunar module pilot's report of particles of light seen through the AOT while looking into a dark quadrant that appeared to move away into space, and a later reported flashing anomaly on the AGS display attributed to electromagnetic interference.

An FBI office memorandum from 1950 summarizes reports of aerial phenomena over sensitive installations in New Mexico since December 1948, categorized into green fireballs, discs, and meteors, with about 150 observations noted and a meteor expert stating the phenomena did not appear to be of meteoric origin.

Two Pan American Airways crew members flying a Constellation aircraft between Gander and La Guardia on 4 August 1947 independently reported sighting an unidentified elongated orange/gold object near Boston, estimated at 15-20 feet long, traveling at 150-175 mph before contact was lost when the aircraft banked or a cloud obscured the view.

A lunar surface photograph shows astronaut shadows in the foreground and a faint blue-white vertical streak of light above the horizon, highlighted via an inset magnification box; the document does not explain the origin of the streak.

An aerial reconnaissance-style image shows a targeting reticle over terrain with a small dark object near the crosshair, but accompanying descriptive text is entirely redacted, leaving no readable account of the observation.
A sensor/camera still with crosshair overlay and timestamp shows a small dark dot near the center of frame against a gray sky background; surrounding text fields are redacted.

A sensor/targeting video still with timestamp 12/31/99 18:10:02 shows a small dark aerial object near reticle markings, with a smaller secondary object below it; no further descriptive text is visible due to redactions.

A USAF mission report (MISREP 5039166) describes a sortie from OKAS supporting NAVCENT operations near the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman, during which the aircrew reported two separate observations of an unidentified aerial phenomenon (UAP) at 2143Z and 2148Z, logged in the mission's observation lines.
A gun-camera or targeting-scope style frame shows a small dark dot near a reticle, with no accompanying text describing the observer, location, or date.

Slide describes multiple sightings by federal law enforcement agents in the western U.S., including groups of red orbs reportedly emitted from a larger orange orb, a hovering glowing orange orb near a rock formation, and a dark kite-shaped object with red and white lights that reportedly moved laterally without changing orientation.
The document shows only a section header labeled '1.4(a)' with no accompanying descriptive text or details about any observation.
A sensor or scope display with crosshair reticle and timestamp shows two small dark spots near the horizontal axis, with surrounding data fields redacted; no descriptive text is visible.
A sensor or targeting display timestamped 12/31/99 18:11:27 shows a small dark point object near crosshairs against a gray background, with surrounding data fields redacted.

A USCENTCOM mission report (MISREP) for a sortie out of OMAM records routine ISR flight activity including takeoff, tasking, and non-interference missions, and notes that during return-to-base at 0457Z the aircraft detected one unidentified aerial phenomenon, with details referenced in an attached UAP entry.

An FBI report from Albuquerque describes a New Mexico State Police officer's account of an object that landed and took off near Socorro on April 24, 1964, leaving four rectangular ground depressions with burned grass and three smaller circular marks nearby.

A sensor display with crosshair grid overlay and timestamp shows a small dark spot near the center; surrounding text fields are redacted, leaving no reported description of the object's appearance or behavior beyond its position on the display.
A radar or sensor-style display with timestamp shows a small dark blip near center crosshairs, with surrounding data fields redacted on the page.
A sensor image dated 12/31/99 18:19:19 shows two small dark blob-like objects near the frame's crosshair marker, with surrounding text fields redacted; no further descriptive detail is available.
A sensor or camera display frame with timestamp and range/reticle overlay shows heavy redaction covering most labeled data fields, leaving no discernible object description in the visible portion.

Emails between analysts discuss administrative approval to add the year (in addition to already-approved month and day) to an unclassified tear line describing a 31 Oct incident in which a U.S. aircraft observed a possible oval/orb-shaped object moving at low speed, tracked for over two hours.

An aircrew debrief form reports that during a night ISR mission on 05/14/20, sensor operators observed a solid white, round object moving over water, which was tracked intermittently with intermittent loss and reacquisition of the sensor lock.

A pilot with squadron HSM-73 reported observing three unidentified round air contacts at night over the North Arabian Sea during routine operations, with negative radar, ES, and IFF track. The contacts were described as maintaining a westerly heading with unknown speed and no interaction occurring with the reporting aircraft.
A sensor or radar display timestamped 12/31/99 18:19:33 shows a small dark blob near the crosshair, with a 3-unit measurement scale marked on either side; surrounding text fields are redacted.
A lunar surface photograph shows a small bright point of light in the sky above the horizon, enlarged in an inset for closer examination; no source or object is identified in the frame.
A sensor/camera still with timestamp shows two small dark dot-like objects near the center crosshair, with a distance scale marker overlaid; surrounding text fields are redacted.

A 2001 State Department cable from the US Embassy in Moscow reports Russian officials denying that Russian aircraft bombed or overflew the Kodori Gorge in Georgia, with an MFA official comparing unverified reports of planes in the area to sightings of UFOs. The cable primarily concerns Russian troop withdrawals and diplomatic exchanges, mentioning the term 'UFO' only as a dismissive comparison used by a Russian official, not as a description of an actual observed phenomenon.

A sensor or camera still shows a small dark object near the center crosshair against a gray background, timestamped 12/31/99 18:18:58, with redacted calibration or identifying panels along the margins.

Two documents: a 1950 Lowry Flight Service Center report describes two spherical objects seen by two named Bendix Aviation employees over Kansas City and Olathe, Kansas, on the night of 6 January 1950, appearing motionless for 10-15 minutes before departing rapidly to the southwest; a 1949 Olmsted Flight Service Center report records a C-45 aircraft sighting near Griffiss Air Force Base, Rome, New York, on 20 September 1949, with limited details filled in on a standard reporting form.

A 1950 letter from a self-described Mexican inventor to a scientific commission in New York discusses stratospheric aerostats, commonly called flying saucers, alongside claims of various unrelated inventions attributed to the author.
A sensor/camera still with a timestamp shows a small dark blob near the center crosshair, with surrounding text fields redacted; no descriptive narrative is visible.

Apollo 11 crew debriefing transcript in which Aldrin, Armstrong, and Collins discuss sighting an unidentified bright object with an L-shape roughly one day out from the moon, initially suspected to be the S-IVB stage, which ground control reported as being 6000 miles away.
A sensor or targeting display with crosshair reticle and timestamp shows a small point-like object against a plain gray background, with surrounding data fields redacted.

An FBI FD-302 report describes an interview with a base contractor who, along with a coworker, observed a large cigar-shaped metallic bronze object with a bright diamond-white light on one end, hovering and slowly moving above a tree line near a test range before disappearing after five to ten seconds. The witness reported no sound, no interference with her vehicle, and no photos or video were taken.

An Apollo-era lunar surface photograph shows an astronaut's shadow in the foreground, with two zoomed inset panels (Area 1 and Area 2) highlighting faint colored specks in the dark sky above the horizon, reportedly examined for anomalous points of light.

An FBI office memorandum from the Cincinnati field office to the Director dated July 22, 1954 concerns Truman Bethurum and reported flying discs, filed under an espionage-related miscellaneous case. It notes enclosed copies of a prior memorandum and additional information provided by a named individual during a July 1954 interview, who was reminded of prior instructions regarding disclosure.

A 482ATKS mission report (Misrep 4871281) describes an aircraft supporting NAVCENT operations over the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman, during which crew received multiple guard calls and, at 1829Z, observed one unidentified anomalous phenomenon (UAP), with full-motion video exploited by DGS-1.
The document shows only a section header, '1.4(a)', with no visible descriptive text or witness account present on the page.

An FBI form letter dated September 6, 1966 from Director J. Edgar Hoover responds to a Mrs. Levi J. Dow of Goffstown, New Hampshire, regarding an inquiry about a flying saucer club, stating the Bureau does not evaluate organizations and has no record of the correspondent or the named group.
An image depicts a smooth, elongated disc-shaped object hovering above a tree line and grassy field, with a bright light or glare emanating from its leading edge.

Two incident summary sheets from a declassified series document ground observations: one near Keithsburg, Iowa on 15 Sept 1948 describing a small metallic disc-shaped object viewed through binoculars drifting south, and another in Ascension Parish, Louisiana on 1 Oct 1948 describing a witness with flying experience observing a cone-shaped, white-hot object flying east in level flight without sound or trail.

An Air Force mission report (89 ATKS, Task Force Chosin) describes a July 31 2022 sortie over an area in the CENTCOM area of responsibility during which the aircrew observed an unidentified aerial phenomenon at a specified grid location before continuing and completing its ISR tasking.

A USAF MISREP (Mission 9337873, 33 SOS) documents an ISR/FMV sortie in which the aircraft crew reported observing one possible unidentified aerial phenomenon during on-station collection; the report references an accompanying UAP annex but does not describe the object's appearance or behavior.

This is an FBI records folder cover and dust jacket for Gray Barker's book 'They Knew Too Much About Flying Saucers,' referencing accounts of researchers who reportedly investigated saucer sightings, including one alleged landing in West Virginia in 1952.

Correspondence bundle consists of a 2001 personal letter from Carol Rosin introducing herself and her husband Jon Cypher ahead of a meeting, and a handwritten hotel-stationery note referencing the French COMETA report, John Callahan's testimony, and air traffic controller Enrique Kolbeck, without describing any specific observed object.

An August 1947 FBI office memo from the San Francisco field office to Director Ladd forwards photostatic copies of Army Air Forces letters and reports on flying disc sightings in Oregon and Arizona, along with earlier correspondence from Fourth Air Force officers at Hamilton Field, California. Copies of the enclosed materials were being forwarded to the Portland and Phoenix field offices for their files on the subject.
This appears to be a sensor or targeting display frame with a timestamp and reticle overlay, showing no clearly described object or event; multiple data fields have been redacted.

An FBI San Francisco office memo from September 1947 forwards an Army Air Force intelligence letter requesting an exhaustive interview with F.M. Johnson of Portland, Oregon regarding his reported sighting of a 'flying disc' on June 24, 1947, noting similarity to Kenneth Arnold's earlier report and possible newspaper influence.
A calibration-style sensor image with grid overlay and timestamp shows a small dark dot near the crosshair origin, with redacted bars along the edges obscuring surrounding data or labels.

A 33 SOS aircrew mission report describes a sortie under ATO 24-024 in which the crew observed an unidentified aerial phenomenon at 0509Z, referenced as UAP 1, during an ISR mission that also included FMV and SIGINT collection tasking.

A 482 ATKS misrep logs a remotely piloted aircraft mission over the Arabian Gulf, Strait of Hormuz, and Gulf of Oman in support of NAVCENT operations, during which the crew reported observing an unidentified aerial phenomenon on three separate occasions during the sortie.
An FBI Dallas office memo dated 11-14-57 refers a matter concerning flying discs, referenced from a Kansas City letter, to OSI without further FBI investigation per existing instructions.

A checklist report describes 1st Lt Joseph C. McHenry at Muroc Air Field, California, on 8 July 1947 sighting two silver disc-like or spherical objects moving at about 300 MPH at roughly 8,000 feet, heading north; other witnesses including S/Sgt Gerald E. Nauman, T/Sgt Joseph Ruvolo, and Miss Jannette Marie Scotte confirmed the sighting, and a third similar object was later reportedly seen performing a tight circle over the airfield.

A pilot from the 77 EFS reported a night-time DCA mission on 10/27/20 in which a radar and targeting pod contact showed two IR-significant objects, one circling the other, that vanished within a fraction of a second. The observer described red blinking strobes and received noise jamming, with the contacts moving at reported speed/direction of 060/20 and unable to be approached closer than 16.9 nautical miles.

An FBI interview report describes witnesses driving to a test site in September 2023 who observed a bright, stationary white light over the horizon that moved to the right and disappeared after about ten seconds; the witness estimated it was ten to twenty miles away and considered it might have been a meteor burning up in the atmosphere.

An October 1947 FBI Portland field office memo describes police officers' accounts of an unidentified object seen over Portland on September 11, 1947, appearing as a bright, silvery, elliptical shape moving faster and higher than a conventional aircraft, with one officer attributing the sighting to sunlight reflecting off a metal airplane.
The document shows only a section header labeled '1.4(a)' with no visible body text or descriptive content about an observed object or event.

An aircrew report from the 172 ATKS describes an infrared sensor tracking a round, cold object over the Gulf of Aden on 15 October 2020, moving at about 20 mph on a heading of 319 degrees with a few abrupt directional changes during roughly one minute of contact.

A sensor or gun-camera style still with crosshair overlay shows a small dark object in flight against a plain sky background, timestamped 12/31/99 18:10:00; surrounding context that might identify location or source has been redacted.
An annotated lunar surface photograph shows an astronaut's shadow on the ground with a small bright point of light in the sky above the horizon, magnified in an inset box for closer examination.

A 1963 NASA Space Council internal memo to a State Department official discusses policy considerations regarding the theoretical possibility of encountering intelligent life in space, referencing scientific and 'flying saucer advocate' viewpoints on Mars, the moon, and planetary formation theory. It does not describe an actual sighting or observed object.
An Apollo-era lunar surface photograph shows a barren landscape with hills on the horizon; an inset zoom of the sky area highlights two small colored points of light against the dark background.

An Air Force pilot's debrief form dated 08/31/20 reports a dusk-time ISR mission sighting of an object at approximately 18,000 feet moving at 150 degrees/230 knots. The pilot describes visually tracking an initial object that was overtaken by another of similar size and shape but much higher speed, with up to three objects observed moving among each other at once.

A series of March 1945 SHAEF and Air Ministry memos discuss reported night phenomena, referred to as 'foo-fighters' and 'balls of fire, red,' observed by Bomber Command and tactical air crews; officials suggest some sightings may have been Me. 262 jets and flak rockets, but state no definite explanation could be given. A related memo notes that photographs of 'long cylindrical objects' claimed by a reconnaissance pilot could not be produced.

A declassified USCENTCOM sensor image, recommended for release by MG Richard A. Harrison, shows a faint elongated light or streak circled in red against a grainy grayscale background; no observer account or location details are provided in the visible text.

A misrep describes an armed overwatch mission near a location in Iraq/Syria coordinates during which the aircrew observed an unidentified aerial phenomenon while conducting a precision-guided munition shot; details of the encounter are referenced but not described in this excerpt.

A 2004 State Department cable from Embassy Ashgabat describes a Turkmen civil society NGO called the Union of UFOlogists of Turkmenabat, noting the group's history assisting local businesses and NGOs with registration; the cable reports the group's president said Turkmen military and government officials had consulted him about unusual occurrences in Turkmen airspace but that there had been no confirmed UFO sightings in Turkmenistan.

A 1994 U.S. Embassy Dushanbe cable reports that Tajik Air's American chief pilot and two colleagues observed a bright, high-speed maneuvering light for about 40 minutes while flying at 41,000 feet over Kazakhstan; they later flew under contrails left by the object, estimated at 100,000 feet, and did not believe it was a meteor.

A 50th ATKS MISREP describes an RPA sortie flying ISR in support of OP Spartan Shield out of Al Dhafra, during which sensor operators reported two visual observations logged as unidentified aerial phenomena at 0241Z and 0322Z; details of the observations are referenced in separate UAP log lines not included in this excerpt.

FBI records compile press clippings on reported sightings: a Michigan pizza shop owner's 1960 color slide photo of a saucer-like object silhouetted against the moon, and a 1959 wire report of an American Airlines pilot and crew describing three bright objects that appeared to trail their plane for 45 minutes near Detroit.

A 1949 FBI office memo from the San Antonio field office to the Director relayed information from Fourth Army G-2 about a Galveston, Texas newspaper article reporting that a business club in Alexandria, Louisiana planned a convention for people who had reported seeing flying saucers, noting two such reports occurred in the area shortly before publication.

An internal correspondence between AFOSI personnel discusses processing a records request as a derivative classification review rather than a full declassification, approving use of an unclassified summary in a product. The document contains no description of an observed aerial object or event.

A radar/sensor display with timestamp 12/31/99 18:20:48 shows a faint dark object near the crosshair center, with surrounding personal or unit identifying information redacted. No further descriptive detail is legible in the visible portion of the frame.

A P-8A aircraft monitoring activity in the Eastern Mediterranean on 18 Nov 2016 reportedly observed an unidentified low-flying object 55 nm northwest of Latakia, Syria, traveling at approximately 500 knots on a southeasterly heading in sea-skim mode before contact was lost about two minutes later near two vessels.

A USCENTCOM mission report describes a reconnaissance aircraft conducting SIGINT collection and target development over an unspecified operating area, during which the crew observed one unidentified aerial phenomenon at 1514Z, documented as a single observation among its taskings.

This is a 1949 Air Force intelligence memorandum (AIRM No. 4) establishing reporting requirements and procedures for sightings of unconventional aircraft and unidentified flying objects, including so-called flying discs. It is an administrative directive rather than a specific sighting report.

This is an administrative cover sheet and correspondence routing document from the Directorate of Intelligence, HQ USAF, dated November 1948, transmitting extracts of a special intelligence organization's reports from the European Command for review; it does not describe an observed aerial object.

A 12 SOS aircraft mission report describes an unspecified aircraft conducting FMV/SIGINT collection sorties over Iraq/Syria on an ISR tasking, noting that at 1559Z the crew observed an unidentified aerial phenomenon referenced as UAP 1, with no further description provided in this narrative section.

A handwritten 1970 letter to James L. Tuck recalls green light sightings observed at Los Alamos between 1948 and 1951, typically between 9 and 11 pm over the Jemez Mountains, along with one afternoon sighting of five objects flying in formation from southeast to northwest; the writer notes these were reported to the Protective Force at the time and suggests checking Protective Force logs for exact dates. A separate related letter from Tuck to a U.S. Army Engineering School requests information on a 'recipe' used for simulated atomic bomb demonstrations, referencing atmospheric vortices described in Edward U. Condon's UFO study.
Pantex ground surveillance radar tower imagery and Sandia National Labs enhanced images depict an unidentified object; supporting descriptive text is redacted.

A club newsletter from Pajarito Astronomers announces a May 1986 meeting featuring a guest speaker discussing why scientists should be concerned about UFO reports; it does not describe any specific sighting or object.

A senior U.S. intelligence officer's report describes a helicopter crew and ground teams observing multiple orange, oval-shaped orbs over a mountain test range at night, some approaching within ten feet of the aircraft, splitting, forming formations, and matching the movements of fighter jets before vanishing.

A CIA field report on the Sary Shagan weapons testing range in the USSR includes an account from a former Soviet source who, in late summer 1973 while stepping outside during a televised sports event, observed a sharp bright green circular mass in the sky west of a test site; within seconds it widened and formed concentric green circles before the coloring faded within minutes, with no associated sound.

An 1100th USAF Special Reporting Group letter dated 7 April 1949 to Sandia Base reports on completed and pending security corrections at Camp Campbell, Kentucky, including gate locks, alert plans, radio installations, and drainage/erosion control efforts; a related folder cover sheet lists the general subject as unidentified flying objects without describing a specific sighting.

An FBI Newark field office file cover and indices search slip references an enclosure of seven photostats depicting flying saucers, received from Department of the Air Force Headquarters on August 27, 1952, with related file cross-references noted by clerks.
An image depicts a nighttime rural road scene with a bright orange orb and three smaller red points of light in the sky above distant hills, with no additional documentary context provided.

An Air Force checklist describes a fireball meteor seen over Norcatur, Kansas on 18 February 1948, reportedly exploding at 30-35 miles altitude with a loud blast followed by smaller explosions and leaving a bluish-white smoke trail; meteorite fragments, including a 109-pound stone, were later recovered from the area.
An illustrative image depicts two silhouetted observers watching a line of small red lights and one larger pale sphere positioned above a mountain ridge at night; no location or date is provided.

An image shows a bright orange orb with a small vertical row of red lights nearby in a dusk sky over a desert road flanked by mountains, with no accompanying textual documentation to establish observer, location, or date.

A 1956 CIA information report from Hungary describes a niece's letter to a naturalized US citizen mentioning fast-moving so-called flying saucers (rockets) seen over several weeks that kept scientists busy, with an estimated speed of 12,000 km/h; a sketch shows a formation of circular objects on a course between Budapest and Moscow.

An FBI FD-302 report describes an interview with a soldier who, along with four others at Fort Carson in early February 2022, observed a motionless, matte white, oval or bean-shaped object hovering over a saddle in Cheyenne Mountain for 3-5 minutes before it vanished. The witness noted the object had intersecting lines forming an abstract polygon pattern and reported no sound, later stating he was contacted by AARO in 2024 but never formally interviewed.

An FBI electronic communication documents contact with a reporting individual who provided videos and described repeated nighttime observations near their residence, appearing as plasma-like orbs, dating back to 2021, sometimes hovering, moving, or near water and trees, along with reported instrument anomalies such as gamma radiation spikes and electronic/GPS interference.

A notional illustration depicts four separate incidents reported by U.S. federal law enforcement agents in the western United States during October 2023, describing orange orbs launching smaller red orbs, a large fiery orb projected against a ridgeline, and thin kite-shaped objects with colored lights observed at close range.

A federal law enforcement agent's narrative to AARO describes multiple sightings over two nights in October 2023 in the western U.S., including bright orb-like lights that expelled smaller red lights in formation, hovering light clusters resembling vehicle headlights, and an orb that accelerated and changed shape while being pursued by car.

This is the cover and preface of a preliminary NASA transcript of the Gemini IV (GT-4) flight crew debriefing, conducted aboard the USS Wasp and concluded at the Manned Spacecraft Center in June 1965; it is an administrative and preface document with no observation account included on these pages.

Two HATS incoming-correspondence tracking records document constituent letters forwarded by U.S. senators (Snowe and Grassley) to the State Department's legal office, one regarding a Maine constituent interested in UFOs and recent Mars photographs, and another regarding a constituent requesting information on UFO sightings reported by astronauts.

This is a draft transcript of an astronaut debriefing discussing dark adaptation and eye patch use during a spaceflight, along with observations of star visibility from orbit compared to expectations. It does not describe an anomalous aerial object but rather routine astronaut observations of stars and night adaptation during a mission.

This is a title/cover page for a CIA history publication on the U-2 and OXCART overhead reconnaissance programs from 1954-1974, authored by Gregory W. Pedlow and Donald E. Welzenbach and published by the CIA History Staff in 1992. It does not describe any observed aerial phenomenon.

A 1967 CIA information report recounts a US astrophysicist's conversations with Soviet astronomers during a trip through the USSR, describing varying levels of interest in and reported sightings of unidentified aerial phenomena, including a reddish flashing object seen near Kiev and reports of ball lightning later attributed to headlight reflections.

A federal law enforcement agent's witness account, provided to AARO, describes observing an orange orb that pulsed between clear and orange and expelled three smaller red orbs over two nights in October 2023 in the western U.S., along with four red lights hovering over an airfield and a vehicle that appeared to evade pursuit before disappearing.

A 1958 CIA memo discusses Dr. Leon Davidson's inquiries about a space message and its transmitter, noting that records were reportedly destroyed by the evaluating agency and that Davidson had not been informed he was dealing with the Agency during earlier contacts.

A 1972 CIA intelligence report summarizes a US citizen's account of a Yerevan, Armenia science conference in September 1971, describing a speculative physics paper by Soviet scientists Kardashev and Sakharov on charged mass collapse in space, and noting a Soviet committee investigating reported flying saucer phenomena was regarded by attendees as lacking scientific rigor.

A CIA information report describes a disc-shaped object with a hollow center and rotating underside lights hovering at high altitude over Harare International Airport, Zimbabwe, on the afternoon of 2 July 2008, reportedly observed by radar and visual means with beams seen emanating from it before it ascended rapidly and left visual range. The incident reportedly led to a facility in Zimbabwe being placed on high alert due to concern over possible foreign surveillance activity.

A 1953 Secretary of Defense routing slip forwards a CIA-transmitted letter and an attached scientific panel report on unidentified flying objects (flying saucers) for review and action, with a handwritten note stating the report appears to warrant action and directing that a copy be sent to the Secretary of the Air Force.

A 1976 CIA Domestic Collection Division report on Soviet aviation medicine describes Aeroflot officials discussing pilot fatigue, biological testing equipment, and radiation research; a section notes a radiation biologist's explanation of cosmic ray particles causing pilots to perceive flashes of light during night flying, and a scientist reportedly stated that 99 percent of UFO occurrences were traceable to natural or man-made phenomena, with one percent unexplained.

FBI Seattle file compiles newspaper clippings from the Columbia Basin News (July 1952) reporting multiple sightings of a bright, fast-moving disc-like object over Pasco and Kennewick, Washington, described by witnesses as ring-shaped with flashing lights, some comparing it to a spinning pinwheel; additional witnesses reported similar objects on subsequent nights.

An AARO memorandum describes six federal law enforcement agents reporting, over two days in October 2023, luminous orange 'mother orbs' near dusk that appeared to release clusters of smaller red 'orbs' exhibiting varied horizontal and altitude movement, with one red orb reportedly stationary for several hours; the report states the case remains unresolved after analysis of flight, radar, and ADS-B data.

NASA memoranda from June 1965 describe the routing and additional flight activities planned for the Gemini IV (GT-4) mission, including extravehicular activity, use of a hand-held maneuvering unit, and a demonstration rendezvous with the spent booster's second stage, which had flashing lights installed for tracking purposes.

A 1956 CIA information report from Hungary describes a naturalized US citizen's niece in Budapest writing that 'flying saucers (rockets)' had been observed speeding overhead for several weeks, causing public unease and prompting scientific investigation, with speeds estimated at 12,000 km/hour; the letter included a hand-drawn sketch showing a formation of circular objects on a course between Budapest and Moscow.

An illustrated scene depicts two silhouetted observers watching a formation of lights in the night sky over a desert hillside, consisting of one larger bright orb and three smaller red points arranged in a line.

An AARO memorandum records a federal law enforcement agent's first-hand account of glowing orb-like lights observed over two nights in October 2023 in the western U.S., including one that appeared to hover near a hillside and emit smaller red lights, plus a separate encounter with a low-flying, translucent object near a roadway.

A billeting officer at Muroc Air Field, California reported seeing two silver spherical or disc-shaped objects flying at about 8000 feet and 300 mph on July 3, 1947, with additional witnesses corroborating the sighting; a third similar object was later seen circling tightly over the airfield.

A 1953 CIA memorandum from the Chief, Physics and Electronics Division reviews the status of the UFOB project, summarizing Air Force, Navy, and Army interest and activities, as well as reported foreign government investigations in Canada, Great Britain, and Sweden, without describing any specific sighting.

A 1950 CIA information report from Chile transmits a translated article by a German scientist, Dr. Eduard Ludwig, proposing an aerodynamic explanation for reported 'flying discs' based on boundary-layer effects, rotating turbines, and Flettner rotor technology developed in German aeronautical research. The article does not describe a specific sighting but offers a theoretical engineering hypothesis for how disc-shaped luminous craft could function.

An image depicts two occupants inside a vehicle at night observing a bright round light along a roadside ahead of them. No accompanying document text is present to establish location, date, or further reported detail.
An image depicts two silhouetted figures observing a small glowing spherical light in the night sky above a desert mountain landscape.

This is a cover and administrative record sheet for Project Blue Book Special Report No. 14, a 1955 Air Technical Intelligence Center document analyzing reports of unidentified aerial objects, without describing a specific sighting itself.
An image shows two silhouetted figures standing in a desert landscape at night, looking toward the sky where a bright round light or moon-like object is visible above distant hills.

An FBI electronic communication describes special agents visiting a witness's home in November 2024 to conduct a site survey of areas where the resident reported observing unidentified aerial phenomena over the past three years, including trail camera footage facing west of the property.

A NASA routing slip and draft transcript from a June 1966 Gemini IX debriefing session discuss science experiments and crew observations, including astronaut Stafford reporting seeing a bright meteor while looking down at Earth, described as whitish-green and moving left to right.

A 1952 CIA memorandum describes British committee activity investigating flying saucer reports, referencing a demonstration at an RAF field in Yorkshire where a 'perfect flying saucer' was reportedly seen by officials and pilots, and mentions related incidents in Sweden and Tremonton. A second page discusses potential dangers such as delayed threat identification, mass hysteria, and communications overload from such sightings.

This is a declassified NASA cover and preface page for the Gemini V (GT-5) flight crew technical debriefing transcript, Part II, prepared by the Spacecraft Operations Branch in September 1965; it contains no description of an observed object or phenomenon.

A 1953 letter set records correspondence between the Federal Civil Defense Administration and the Intelligence Advisory Committee regarding a scientific panel's report on unidentified flying objects, forwarding conclusions and recommendations for public education and civil defense planning.

Australian Department of Defence intelligence minute (1971) discusses attaching two documents examining U.S. government handling of UFO reports, including alleged CIA/USAF involvement in Project Blue Book and claims about possible propulsion research, plus a separate compilation of reported evidence of weapon systems associated with UFO sightings.

A 1949 letter from a McMinnville, Oregon minister to FBI Director J. Edgar Hoover describes an observation from the prior May of four narrow beams of light seen converging over the Cascade mountains, with small clouds forming within the beams and an apparent explosion effect at their convergence point rising to about ten thousand feet, visible for at least ten minutes. Hoover's reply thanks the writer for the information without further comment.

An FBI electronic communication describes agents conducting a site survey around a redacted location in December 2024, following up on a UAP sighting reported by two agents in November 2024. The survey team walked the perimeter and inspected areas with prior sightings but found nothing unusual on the ground or at treetop level, noting moderate tree cover that would make nighttime drone or craft flight there difficult.

A federal law enforcement agent's field narrative to AARO describes multiple sightings over two nights in October 2023 in the western U.S., including a stationary orange light over a ridge, a large red orb releasing smaller red orbs, and a paired red-and-white light near a road that hovered, moved, and later resolved into a faint formation of lights seen with night vision goggles.
An image depicts two silhouetted witnesses standing beside a car in a desert landscape at night, reportedly observing a line of red lights and separate pairs of white lights above distant mountains.
An image shows two silhouetted figures at night observing a single point of light in the sky near a mountain horizon; no accompanying document text is present to describe the observation in detail.

A 1949 Department of the Army routing/cover sheet from the Plans & Operations Division records an evaluation study concerning reported phenomena described as 'flying saucers,' routed for action to the Director of Intelligence with no further details of specific sightings included on this form.

An FBI FD-302 interview report describes two witnesses in a residential backyard observing a red spherical object with a bright white center that hovered, changed altitude and direction, and appeared to merge with a second identical orb before moving out of sight; weeks later smaller white orbs were reportedly seen passing overhead at higher altitude.

A CIA field report on the Sary Shagan weapons testing range in the USSR includes an account of a source who, in late summer 1973, observed a bright green circular mass in the western sky that widened and formed concentric circles before fading within minutes, with no associated sound.

An FBI FD-302 report describes witnesses observing a yellow, basketball-sized ball of light and a larger beach-ball-sized light near trees behind their property around 9:15pm, which appeared to move laterally and merge before disappearing; the account also mentions later sightings of white orbs and a separate 1987 incident involving a red light near power lines.

A U.S. Army report describes five service members at Fort Carson, Colorado observing a stationary, slowly shape-shifting airborne object above Cheyenne Mountain for 30–180 seconds on 15 February 2022; the object had a translucent, shimmering white appearance and disappeared suddenly, with analysts assessing low confidence that it was possible sunlight backscattering off snow and clouds.

An FBI electronic communication documents an interview with a redacted witness who described repeated sightings of lights near their property since 2021, including an initial 2021 sighting where a single light split into two or three lights forming a triangular pattern, plus a separate 1996 incident of a large light stopping a vehicle in New Mexico. The witness described cross-referencing sightings with ADS-B flight data to rule out aircraft and stated activity increased since September.

A 1968 CIA information report describes multiple sightings of unidentified flying objects over Ladakh, Nepal, Sikkim, and Bhutan between February and March 1968, compiling witness observations of lights and bright objects seen in the sky. One entry from March 4, 1968 near Chang La, Fukche, and Koyul describes a white light accompanied by two blasting sounds, followed by a reddish light and white smoke.

This declassified NASA document is a preliminary transcript of the GT-4 (Gemini 4) flight crew debriefing conducted aboard the recovery ship USS Wasp on June 9, 1965, covering the mission from an operational viewpoint. It is administrative in nature and does not describe any aerial phenomenon sighting in the visible portion.
A form-based government document, largely illegible due to poor scan quality, appears to record procedural or policy guidance rather than a specific witness sighting account.

A memorandum from AARO records the narrative of Witness 4, a federal law enforcement agent, describing multiple nights in October 2023 in the western U.S. during which they observed numerous orange, yellow, and white lights over hillsides and ridgelines, some appearing to cluster, form geometric patterns, split into smaller lights, or move rapidly between mountain terrain without visible aircraft or vehicles.
An image depicts a bright orange spherical object glowing above a desert mountain ridge near a roadway at night. No accompanying document text is present to describe an observer, date, or location.
An image depicts a disc-shaped object with a mottled, textured surface hovering in the sky above a mountainous area with a residential foothill community below.

This is the cover and preface of a declassified NASA technical debriefing for the Gemini VII mission, dated December 23, 1965, documenting crew debriefing sessions held December 19-21, 1965 at Cape Kennedy, Florida. The document contains no reported sighting of an anomalous object.

A CIA information report from a US traveler describes an October 1955 train journey between Baku and Tiflis in the Azerbaijan SSR, during which the source and companions observed a triangular object with three lights near an airfield, illuminated by a searchlight, that reportedly performed spiraling maneuvers before climbing rapidly at a steep angle; a train steward later drew the compartment blinds after apparently being instructed by an accompanying MVD official.

An FBI electronic communication describes two special agents visiting a witness's home in November 2024 to confirm prior reports of aerial phenomena observed over three years. During the visit, agents reportedly observed pulsating white and blue/white lights near the tree line that moved erratically before disappearing, and photographs taken were described as mostly blurry with little contextual detail.

An FBI electronic communication describes a forensic sketch interview of a former U.S. Army intelligence officer who, with four colleagues, reported observing an unidentified aerial phenomenon over the Cheyenne Mountains area in February. The witness described a potato-shaped, translucent, whitish object made of shifting scale-like panels that remained stationary for about two minutes before vanishing abruptly with no shadow.

A December 1948 Navy memorandum from the Fifth Naval District forwards a Chief of Naval Operations directive instructing all naval districts to report sightings of 'flying discs' or other unidentified aerial objects to the nearest Air Force command and Naval District Intelligence Office, including photographic evidence when possible, due to an anticipated new cycle of reported sightings.

This is the cover and preface of a declassified NASA Gemini V technical debriefing transcript (Part 1), prepared by the Spacecraft Operations Branch in September 1965, summarizing crew accounts of the mission from an operational viewpoint; it does not itself describe any anomalous object.
